name: Bob Straub State Park
short_description: Coastal state park near Tillamook offering beach and tidal zone rockhounding for agate and chalcedony. No collection allowed within park boundaries; nearby public lands offer better options.
description:
Bob Straub State Park is a scenic coastal park in Tillamook County featuring sandy beaches and rocky outcrops along the Oregon coast. The park sits at sea level with excellent views of tide pools and coastal formations. While the park itself prohibits rock and mineral collection per state park rules, the surrounding Tillamook area offers nearby opportunities on BLM and public lands where agate, chalcedony, and jasper can be found in coastal gravels and beach deposits. The park makes an excellent basecamp for exploring the wider rockhounding region. The low elevation and proximity to the Pacific Ocean create consistent, cool conditions year-round. Visitors should plan to explore nearby public lands and beaches outside park boundaries for actual collecting.

The Oregon coast sits within a zone of significant Tertiary volcanic and sedimentary activity. Basalt flows, rhyolite deposits, and silica-rich mineral formations from the Cascade and Coastal Range provinces transported down to coastal areas by ancient river systems have created excellent conditions for agate and chalcedony nodule formation. These minerals weathered from volcanic parent rock and concentrated in beach gravels and alluvial deposits over millions of years.

The tidal zone near Bob Straub offers exposure of different geological layers during low tide, revealing bands of volcanic rock, sediment, and weathered mineral concentrations. Nearby creeks and stream beds that flow into the Pacific often carry agates and other silicate minerals weathered from upland volcanic sources. The coastal environment continuously exposes fresh material through wave action and erosion.
history: The Oregon coast formed during the Tertiary Period through volcanic activity related to the Cascade volcanic arc and subduction zone geology. Miocene and Pliocene rhyolitic and basaltic eruptions deposited silica-rich materials that later weathered and concentrated into agate nodules and chalcedony deposits now found in coastal gravels and stream beds throughout Tillamook County.
tips: Focus on the tidal zone and rocky outcrops visible during low tide - this is where wave action exposes fresh agate and chalcedony specimens. Search in the black sand and gravel deposits where volcanic minerals naturally concentrate. Look for translucent or opaque stones with smooth, worn surfaces and banded or mottled patterns typical of coastal agates. Bring a good flashlight and loupe to identify banding patterns and quality, and always check tide tables before exploring rocky areas - never get caught by incoming tides.
terrain_notes: Elevation 7 feet at sea level. Terrain consists of sandy beach, tidal flats, and scattered rocky outcrops. Walking surface is sand and fine gravel with some loose rocks and tide pools. Access to beach areas is generally easy, but rocky intertidal zones can be slippery with algae and seaweed. No elevation gain. Vegetation is minimal due to coastal exposure - primarily beach grasses and salt-tolerant plants.
best_season: April - September. Summer and early fall offer the most stable weather, lower rainfall, and better visibility. Winter storms and high surf can make beach access dangerous and reduce visibility.
written_directions: From Tillamook, take US 101 north for approximately 7 miles toward Pacific City. Turn right (east) on Brooten Road and continue for about 3 miles. Follow signs to Bob Straub State Park parking area on the right. The park entrance is well marked from US 101.
road_conditions: Brooten Road is paved and well maintained. Parking area is paved.
parking_notes: Dedicated state park parking lot with capacity for 40+ vehicles. Well maintained, free parking.
collection_rules: No collection of rocks, minerals, or other natural materials is permitted within Bob Straub State Park boundaries. Rock and mineral collecting is prohibited to protect the park's natural resources. Rockhounders should explore nearby BLM and public lands outside the park for actual collecting opportunities.
rules:
No rock or mineral collection allowed
No removal of any natural materials
Stay within park boundaries
Follow tide table safety rules
Respect posted closures and restrictions
nearest_services: Tillamook, 10 miles south - gas, food, lodging, and supplies available
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat terrain at sea level with easy beach access. Paved parking and maintained park facilities. Sandy beach walking is accessible to most fitness levels. Rocky tide pool areas may be slippery and uneven - sturdy footwear with good grip recommended. Tide pool exploration requires caution and tide table awareness. No significant elevation change.

faq.question: Can I collect agate and chalcedony at Bob Straub State Park?
faq.answer: No - collection of rocks and minerals is not permitted within Bob Straub State Park boundaries. The park prohibits removal of all natural materials to protect the coastal ecosystem. However, nearby BLM lands and public beaches outside the park often have agate and chalcedony deposits available for recreational collecting under Oregon's 25 lb per day limit.

faq.question: What gemstones can I find rockhounding near Bob Straub State Park?
faq.answer: The Tillamook area and nearby coastal zones contain agate, chalcedony, and jasper. These silicate minerals weather from Tertiary volcanic rocks in the Cascade and Coastal Ranges and concentrate in beach gravels and stream deposits along the Oregon coast. Tidal zones and coastal streams offer the best exposure during low tide.

faq.question: What is the best season for rockhounding near Tillamook?
faq.answer: April through September offers the best conditions. Summer months provide stable weather, lower rainfall, better visibility for searching, and optimal tide conditions for exploring the tidal zone. Winter storms increase wave action and reduce safe access to rocky outcrops.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to collect rocks on public lands near Bob Straub?
faq.answer: No permit is required for recreational rockhounding on BLM lands near Bob Straub. Oregon allows personal use collection of up to 25 lbs per day under FLPMA rules. No commercial collection is allowed. Always check which lands are public BLM or state lands before collecting, and avoid the state park itself.

faq.question: Is Bob Straub State Park good for families and beginners?
faq.answer: Yes - the park itself is beginner-friendly with easy beach access, reliable cell service, and nearby services in Tillamook. However, note that no collecting is allowed in the park. The park makes an excellent basecamp for exploring nearby rockhounding sites on public lands. Kids ages 6+ will enjoy tide pool exploration and learning about coastal geology.

faq.question: How far is Bob Straub State Park from Tillamook?
faq.answer: Bob Straub State Park is approximately 10 miles north of Tillamook via US 101 and Brooten Road. The drive takes about 15-20 minutes. Tillamook offers all services including gas, food, lodging, and supplies for your rockhounding trip.
